sections.title: Use
sections.description: Certain verbs only require a subject to form a complete sentence. No object is needed.

sections.title: Use
sections.description: A large number of verbs take a single object after them to complete their meaning.

sections.title: Use
sections.description: Certain verbs can take two objects at the same time. One is the direct object (the thing) and the other is the indirect object (usually a person).

sections.title: Useful notes
items: She bought a gift **for** her brother.

sections.description: These verbs can also be used in the passive.

sections.title: Be careful
items: When the indirect object is placed before the direct object, do not add a preposition.
Correct: She gave Leo the keys.
Incorrect: She gave to Leo the keys.

exercises.exerciseTypeName: A: Complete with the correct form
exercises.title: A: Complete with the correct form
exercises.instructions: Complete each sentence with one suitable verb.
exercises.studentInstruction: Complete each sentence with one suitable verb.

items.sentence: After dinner, Grandpa ...... in his chair.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is past simple irregular, so the correct answer is "slept".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Present perfect does not combine with finished past-time words like yesterday or last week.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Past simple is used for finished actions at a specific past time.

items.sentence: I ...... my backpack next to the door.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is past simple irregular, so the correct answer is "put".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Present perfect does not combine with finished past-time words like yesterday or last week.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Past simple is used for finished actions at a specific past time.

items.sentence: Can you ...... me your phone charger?

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is past simple irregular, so the correct answer is "lend".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Present perfect does not combine with finished past-time words like yesterday or last week.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Past simple is used for finished actions at a specific past time.

items.sentence: Jake ...... the door quietly when he left.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is past simple irregular, so the correct answer is "closed".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Present perfect does not combine with finished past-time words like yesterday or last week.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Past simple is used for finished actions at a specific past time.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is past simple irregular, so the correct answer is "laughed".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Past simple is wrong when the sentence links past to now (for, since, already, yet, ever).
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present perfect links a past action to the present or unfinished time.

items.sentence: Mum ...... me a text message this morning.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is past simple irregular, so the correct answer is "sent".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Present perfect does not combine with finished past-time words like yesterday or last week.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Past simple is used for finished actions at a specific past time.

exercises.title: B: Choose the correct sentence
exercises.instructions: Choose the sentence that is grammatically correct.
exercises.studentInstruction: Choose the sentence that is grammatically correct.

options: Emma gave Jack a postcard.
Emma gave to Jack a postcard.
items.displayPrompt: A Emma gave Jack a postcard. / B Emma gave to Jack a postcard.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "A" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "A" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "Emma gave Jack a postcard." would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

"Emma gave Jack a postcard." does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence.

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

"Emma gave Jack a postcard." doesn't fit here → "A" does, because it does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence.
"Emma gave to Jack a postcard." doesn't fit here → "A" does, because Only "A" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.

options: I bought my cousin some headphones.
I bought for my cousin some headphones.
items.displayPrompt: A I bought my cousin some headphones. / B I bought for my cousin some headphones.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "A" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "A" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "I bought my cousin some headphones." would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

"I bought my cousin some headphones." does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence.

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

"I bought my cousin some headphones." doesn't fit here → "A" does, because it does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence.
"I bought for my cousin some headphones." doesn't fit here → "A" does, because Only "A" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.

"Dad showed us his holiday photos." doesn't fit here → "A" does, because it does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence.
"Dad showed to us his holiday photos." doesn't fit here → "A" does, because Only "A" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.

exercises.title: C: Choose the correct sentence
exercises.instructions: Choose the sentence that uses to or for correctly. Click A or B.
exercises.studentInstruction: Choose the sentence that uses to or for correctly. Tap A or B.

options: A: Ella gave the keys to her brother.
B: Ella gave the keys for her brother.

"A: Ella gave the keys to her brother." does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence.

"B: Ella gave the keys for her brother." doesn't fit here → "A" does, because Only "A" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.

B: I wrote an email to my teacher.

"A: I wrote an email for my teacher." does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence.

optionContrast.A: The company offered a discount to cus: "A: The company offered a discount to customers." doesn't fit here → "A" does, because it does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence.

exercises.exerciseTypeName: D: Gap fill (direct and indirect objects)
exercises.title: D: Gap fill (direct and indirect objects)
exercises.instructions: Write one word in each gap.
exercises.studentInstruction: Write one word in each gap.

items.sentence: Could you pass ...... the bread, please?

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is indirect object, so the correct answer is "me".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check whether the noun is specific, countable, or mentioned for the first time.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

items.sentence: Can you explain the homework ...... me again?

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Explain to me again.
explanationContent.commonMistake: Try saying the phrase aloud — the correct preposition is usually part of a fixed pattern.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the full sentence with your answer inserted — if it sounds wrong, check tense, preposition, or word form.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: My father taught ...... how to swim.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is double object, so the correct answer is "me".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Try saying the phrase aloud — the correct preposition is usually part of a fixed pattern.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: We paid ...... cashier before leaving the shop.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is direct object, so the correct answer is "the".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check whether the noun is specific, countable, or mentioned for the first time.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

items.sentence: She sent an invitation ...... her grandparents.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Send to her grandparents.
explanationContent.commonMistake: Try saying the phrase aloud — the correct preposition is usually part of a fixed pattern.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the full sentence with your answer inserted — if it sounds wrong, check tense, preposition, or word form.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: Could you make a sandwich ...... me?

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Make for me a sandwich.
explanationContent.commonMistake: Try saying the phrase aloud — the correct preposition is usually part of a fixed pattern.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the full sentence with your answer inserted — if it sounds wrong, check tense, preposition, or word form.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

exercises.title: E: Error correction
exercises.instructions: Each sentence has one mistake. Write the correct sentence.
exercises.studentInstruction: Each sentence has one mistake. Write the correct sentence.

items.displayPrompt: Lucy gave to Ben her old laptop.

cues: Lucy gave to Ben her old laptop.

items.answer: Lucy gave Ben her old laptop.
acceptedAnswers: Lucy gave Ben her old laptop.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: No to with double object.
explanationContent.sentenceBreakdown: Lucy gave Ben her old laptop.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Build a grammatically complete sentence that matches the prompt: Lucy gave Ben her old laptop.

wrongOptions.No: Check how "No" changes in the target sentence — tense or word form may shift.
wrongOptions.to: Check how "to" changes in the target sentence — tense or word form may shift.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Match the tense shift shown in the explanation — one verb often moves back one step in time.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Write one complete sentence with correct punctuation — capital letter at the start and a full stop at the end.
explanationContent.question: Lucy gave <mistake>to</mistake> Ben her old laptop.
explanationContent.fix: "to" → "Lucy gave Ben her old laptop." (expand to "Lucy gave Ben her old laptop.")
explanationContent.correctedSentence: Lucy gave Ben her old laptop.

items.displayPrompt: Dad bought to me a bicycle.

cues: Dad bought to me a bicycle.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Buy + person + thing.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Build a grammatically complete sentence that matches the prompt: Dad bought me a bicycle.

wrongOptions.Buy: Check how "Buy" changes in the target sentence — tense or word form may shift.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Match the tense shift shown in the explanation — one verb often moves back one step in time.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Write one complete sentence with correct punctuation — capital letter at the start and a full stop at the end.
explanationContent.question: Dad bought <mistake>to</mistake> me a bicycle.
explanationContent.fix: "to" → "Dad bought me a bicycle." (use the correct quantifier or article)

items.displayPrompt: Please show me to the manager.

cues: Please show me to the manager.

items.answer: Please show the manager to me.
acceptedAnswers: Please show the manager to me.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Show person + thing.
explanationContent.sentenceBreakdown: Please show the manager to me.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Build a grammatically complete sentence that matches the prompt: Please show the manager to me.

wrongOptions.Show: Check how "Show" changes in the target sentence — tense or word form may shift.
wrongOptions.person: Check how "person" changes in the target sentence — tense or word form may shift.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Match the tense shift shown in the explanation — one verb often moves back one step in time.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Write one complete sentence with correct punctuation — capital letter at the start and a full stop at the end.
explanationContent.question: Please show <mistake>me to the manager.</mistake>
explanationContent.fix: "me to the manager." → "the manager to me." (use the correct quantifier or article)
explanationContent.correctedSentence: Please show the manager to me.

items.displayPrompt: They offered to him a free ticket.

cues: They offered to him a free ticket.

items.answer: They offered him a free ticket.
acceptedAnswers: They offered him a free ticket.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Offer + person + thing.
explanationContent.sentenceBreakdown: They offered him a free ticket.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Build a grammatically complete sentence that matches the prompt: They offered him a free ticket.

wrongOptions.Offer: Check how "Offer" changes in the target sentence — tense or word form may shift.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Match the tense shift shown in the explanation — one verb often moves back one step in time.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Write one complete sentence with correct punctuation — capital letter at the start and a full stop at the end.
explanationContent.question: They offered <mistake>to</mistake> him a free ticket.
explanationContent.fix: "to" → "They offered him a free ticket." (use the correct quantifier or article)
explanationContent.correctedSentence: They offered him a free ticket.

items.displayPrompt: I wrote to my cousin a long email.

cues: I wrote to my cousin a long email.

items.answer: I wrote my cousin a long email.
acceptedAnswers: I wrote my cousin a long email.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Write + person + thing.
explanationContent.sentenceBreakdown: I wrote my cousin a long email.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Build a grammatically complete sentence that matches the prompt: I wrote my cousin a long email.

wrongOptions.Write: Check how "Write" changes in the target sentence — tense or word form may shift.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Match the tense shift shown in the explanation — one verb often moves back one step in time.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Write one complete sentence with correct punctuation — capital letter at the start and a full stop at the end.
explanationContent.question: I wrote <mistake>to</mistake> my cousin a long email.
explanationContent.fix: "to" → "I wrote my cousin a long email." (use the correct quantifier or article)
explanationContent.correctedSentence: I wrote my cousin a long email.

items.displayPrompt: The teacher read to the students the story.

cues: The teacher read to the students the story.

items.answer: The teacher read the students the story.
acceptedAnswers: The teacher read the students the story.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Read + person + thing.
explanationContent.sentenceBreakdown: The teacher read the students the story.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Build a grammatically complete sentence that matches the prompt: The teacher read the students the story.

wrongOptions.Read: Check how "Read" changes in the target sentence — tense or word form may shift.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Match the tense shift shown in the explanation — one verb often moves back one step in time.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Write one complete sentence with correct punctuation — capital letter at the start and a full stop at the end.
explanationContent.question: The teacher read <mistake>to</mistake> the students the story.
explanationContent.fix: "to" → "The teacher read the students the story." (expand to "The teacher read the students the story.")
explanationContent.correctedSentence: The teacher read the students the story.

exercises.title: F: Key word transformation
exercises.instructions: Complete the second sentence so that it has a similar meaning.
exercises.studentInstruction: Complete the second sentence so that it has a similar meaning.

items.sentence:
I bought my dad a magazine. **FOR**
I bought a magazine ...... dad.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is rewrite with for, so the correct answer is "for my".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "for my", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence:
She sent a message to Amy. **SENT**
She ...... message.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is double object, so the correct answer is "sent Amy a".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "sent Amy a", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Some verbs take two objects — a person (indirect) and a thing (direct).

items.sentence:
Could you give this book to me? **ME**
Could you ...... book?

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is double object, so the correct answer is "give me this".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "give me this", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Some verbs take two objects — a person (indirect) and a thing (direct).

items.sentence:
He cooked dinner for us. **COOKED**
He ...... dinner.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is double object, so the correct answer is "cooked us".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "cooked us", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence:
The guide showed the paintings to the visitors. **SHOWED**
The guide ...... paintings.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is double object, so the correct answer is "showed the visitors the".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "showed the visitors the", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

items.sentence:
Don't send your address to strangers. **SEND**
Don't ...... address.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is double object, so the correct answer is "send strangers your".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "send strangers your", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Some verbs take two objects — a person (indirect) and a thing (direct).
